experiments.title: How increasing the urgency impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the urgency and need for rural education donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2025 - 07/08/2025
During the Make Summer Fair campaign, we wanted to test whether increasing the urgency of the need on the donation form value prop copy, specifically tied to rural education, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether shifting rural education messaging and imagery to emphasize need — rather than happiness — would motivate more donors to give, and to give higher amounts.
Key Results:
Overall conversion dropped by 20% at 97% confidence — a statistically significant decline. RPV decreased by 6%, though that result wasn't statistically significant. The average gift, however, increased by $13.
The decrease in conversions outweighed the increase in average gift size, resulting in a net negative impact on overall performance.
Performance by Segment:
On desktop, conversion fell 23% while RPV nudged up 16% — suggesting the more urgent messaging resonated with a subset of desktop donors willing to give more, even as it turned others away. Mobile told a similar story: conversion dropped 15%, and RPV fell 21%, indicating the imagery may have been particularly off-putting on smaller screens where it's more prominent.
One-time giving saw a 19% drop in conversion at the 93% confidence level. Recurring giving declined across the board — conversion down 24% and RPV down 46% — though the sample wasn't large enough to draw firm conclusions there.
What the Results Suggest
The messaging didn't connect with most donors, but for those it did reach, it encouraged higher gifts. This suggests a segmentation opportunity rather than a broad messaging shift — the tone of urgency may work for a specific audience but feels less effective for the majority.
Next Steps:
Rather than abandoning the approach, we'll continue iterating to find a version that captures the average gift lift without the conversion drop. For example, we'll test a softer framing of need that feels compelling without being off-putting.

experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donate button on mobile impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for mobile donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2025 - 10/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button for mobile users. We hypothesized that showing the donation amount at the point of conversion would reduce friction by reinforcing the user’s selection and thus would increase conversion rate.
We had run this test earlier in the year with different results. For this experiment, we ran it on a Gaza-specific donation form that was getting traffic due to recent news in the area.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test assessed whether showing the donation amount on the "Donate" button increased mobile donor confidence and completion rates.
Key Results:
The conversion lift was statistically significant — the overall conversion rate increased by 37% at the 99% confidence level. Revenue Per Visitor (RPV) also trended upward by 12%; however, this change was not statistically significant, as it did not reach the 95% confidence threshold.
What the Results Suggest:
Displaying the amount on the button helps mobile donors commit with more confidence.

experiments.title: How defaulting a donation module to monthly giving impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to monthly for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2025 - 08/13/2025

experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. Defaulting to monthly donations led to a significant increase in conversions for potential donors.

2. The treatment group saw a 9.7% increase in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.5%).

How to Use What Was Learned in Future Experimentation:

1. Consider defaulting to monthly donations in future donation modules to potentially improve conversion rates.

2. Continue to experiment with different variables such as donation amounts, payment methods, and messaging to further optimize conversion rates.

3. Monitor and analyze the results of future experiments to continuously improve the donation process and maximize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visual call out for monthly giving for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2026 - 09/26/2026
With monthly giving a big focus for Save the Children, we want to test whether a graphic call-out highlighting the impact of monthly giving would increase conversion rates. We ran 3 different variants for this test, only for desktop traffic (since the buttons stacked on mobile):
Control
Variant 1- monthly call out text below the monthly/one-time array button
Variant 2- monthly call out text above the monthly/one-time array button
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding a visual monthly callout on the donation form. We hypothesized that this would strengthen the proposition for monthly giving.
Key Results:
This test showed that the visual monthly callout made the recurring option more salient and easier to process. By reducing cognitive friction and highlighting it as a “default-worthy” choice, users were nudged to see monthly as the standard, or even preferred, behavior.
Rather than deterring one-time donors, the callout acted as a commitment anchor. Donors compared themselves against the recurring option and felt reassured in choosing either. The result is a “lift all boats” effect: greater clarity increases confidence, which increases giving.
This suggests that visual emphasis on giving types does more than shift behavior between options. It can grow the whole pie by lowering decision friction and reinforcing giving as the normative action.

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover (redirect) impacts conversion rates on Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a homepage takever for potential donors on Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2025 - 12/02/2025
For Save the Children US, Giving Tuesday has historically been one of the most important fundraising days of the year. This year, we wanted to test whether using a homepage takeover–where a user visiting the main homepage would instantly be redirected to a donation page featuring Giving Tuesday language–could increase conversion rates. The control had no takeover, and the treatment was set to show once per user per day, and featured a ‘continue to savethechildren.org’ link at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of redirecting homepage traffic to a donation form on a high-volume giving day. We hypothesized that this would increase conversion by reducing friction for those who were looking to donate already, as well as help nudge those users on the fence of donating.
Key Results:
-       +14% statistically significant increase (96%) in overall conversion rate.
-       +12% non-statistically significant increase (84%) in overall RPV.
The results confirm that during peak giving times, homepage visitors include a high share of donation-intent users. Directing them straight to the donation form reduced friction and choice overload, elevating focus on the single task of giving and better aligning with user motivation.
Specific Results:
Overall performance when looking at transaction conversion rates had a 14% increase with a 96% level of confidence. In terms of revenue per visitor, the homepage takeover saw a 12% increase with an 83% level of confidence.
Desktop vs. Mobile:
Desktop:
Control: 19% conversion rate
Treatment: 22% conversion rate, which is a 15% increase with a 95% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 16% with an 87% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 13% conversion rate
Treatment: 14.2% conversion rate, which is an 8% increase in conversion rate with a 55% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor only increased by 2.3%, with a 54% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis for this big difference between desktop and mobile is that on mobile most potential donors are presented with a donation module at the top of the page, and therefore did not see a radically different experience like desktop users did.
Since Save is focusing on increasing recurring gifts, the emphasis on both control and treatment was pre-selecting monthly gifts. Therefore, it is interesting to look at whether the takeover had an impact on those:
One-time gifts vs. recurring
Recurring:
Control had a 1.2% conversion rate
Treatment: 1.1% conversion rate, which is a 9% decrease with a 29% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 28% with an 82% level of confidence
One-time:
Control had a 15% conversion rate.
Treatment: 17.6% conversion rate, which is a 16% increase with a 97% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 14% with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a more modernized donation form design impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a more modern design for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/13/2025 - 11/21/2025
Save the Children US has been using the same donation form design for a number of years, so we wanted to test whether a more modern look and improved visibility of key features would increase conversion rates by making the experience more appealing and accessible to potential donors. We ran this experiment on their evergreen donation form so as to not impact any campaign-specific results.
Instead of testing every single element to get to a ‘best practices’ form, we decided to test a major redesign first, and then in subsequent tests look at how changes to each element of the form impacted conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of redesigning the main donation form. We hypothesized that a modernized design, combined with improved visibility of the form, would increase conversion by making the experience more appealing and accessible to donors.
Key Results:
-       47% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.
-       200% statistically significant increase (100%) in overall RPV.
The results show a directional increase in overall conversion and RPV, suggesting that the improved design and visibility of the donation form encouraged more users to complete a donation.
Overall results:
Control: 25% conversion rate
Treatment: 37% conversion rate, which is a 47% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased dramatically, with a 200% increase and a 99% level of confidence.
Desktop versus mobile:
Since the biggest design changes were on desktop (the mobile version of the new form stacked similarly to the control) we expected the biggest changes on desktop:
Desktop:
Control: 27% conversion rate
Treatment: 41% conversion rate, which is a 48% increase with a 98% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 225% with a 99% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 21% conversion rate
Treatment: 26% conversion rate, which was a 22% increase with only a 43% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor on mobile also increased slightly, with an 8% increase, but only a 56% level of confidence.
One-time versus recurring:
Recurring:
Control: 5.9% conversion rate
Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 28% decrease with a 56% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased, however, by 91% with a 76% level of confidence (the donation sample size, however, was low, with a larger gift skewing this metric).
One-time:
Control: 19.5% conversion rate.
Treatment: 33% conversion rate, which is a 70% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 211% with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donation button impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for potential donors will achieve change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2025 - 06/16/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button. Our hypothesis was that adding the amount to the button when people went to submit their donation would remove some of the anxiety when donating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:

-       +31% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.

-       -25% non-statistically significant decline (85%) in overall RPV.

-       -70% statistically significant decline (99%) in recurring donor conversion rate.

-       -46% non-statistically significant decline (94%) in recurring RPV.

Although there was a lift in overall conversion, driven by mobile users, there was a significant decline in revenue. This test suggests that calling out the donation total on the last step causes friction for certain audiences and also gives them another opportunity to question their giving amount.

Recommendation/Implementation:

Given our priority for recurring giving, we will not be implementing the variant.

Treatment: 53% conversion rate--a 47% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor decreased by 24%, with an 82% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a micro-ask decision in an emergency banner impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-ask decision in an emergency banner for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2024 - 07/24/2024
This test explored the impact of a micro ask to the emergency banner on an emergency page. The goal was to increase conversion by encouraging the user to engage with the banner. Our hypothesis was that adding a micro-ask decision in the emergency banner will effectively increase the overall conversion rates. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling. Incorporating a micro-ask can make users more involved and attentive toward the emergency banner.
We had 3 different variants of this micro-ask, which we had originally tested in a previous experiment.
Variant A: Changed “Donate Today” to “Yes, I would like to Donate.” Added a button with the text “No, I’m not interested in making a donation.”
Variant B: Changed “Donate Today” to “Yes, I would like to Donate.” Added a button with the text “No, I’m not interested in making a donation.” Changed both button colors to white with red text.
Variant C: Changed “Donate Today” to “Yes, I would like to Donate.” Added a button with the text “No, I’m not interested in making a donation.” Changed “Yes” button color to white with red text.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
Overall, all variants saw a significant increase in button clicks at a statistically significant confidence level.
 	On desktop, all variants saw an increase in donor conversion at a statistically significant confidence level.
 	On mobile, all variants saw a decrease in both donor conversion and revenue per visitor at a statistically significant confidence level.

experiments.title: How placing a donation module on the homepage impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation module for website visitors will achieve increase conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2025 - 04/16/2025
This test explored whether adding a donation module to the hero image on the homepage of Save the Children USA increased conversion over the standard top-right donate button. Our hypothesis was that the module will reduce friction for the user by presenting the donation levels at the start of the process–rather than the user clicking on the donate button and then going to the form–and also splitting the donation process into 2 smaller steps. This initial experiment was run during a 10x match campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donation module to the hero image on the homepage of Save the Children USA significantly increased conversion rates. The treatment group, which saw the donation module, experienced a 54.2% increase in donations compared to the control group with a high level of statistical confidence (99.3%).
Moreover, there was a 72% increase in revenue per visitor on the treatment page, with a 97% level of confidence.
The increase in conversion rate reflects reduced friction in the customer journey. The module on the hero provides the user with an easy way to give while also breaking up the donating process by providing the gifting level and amount up front. Then the user just has to complete their billing information as a secondary step. The module also allows us to provide equivalencies for each giving level which helps to make the impact more tangible for the donor.
Key metrics:
Desktop overall performance:
Control: 2% conversion rate
Treatment: 3.1% conversion rate, which is a 56% increase at a 95% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 39%, with an 80% level of confidence (one large gift removed from the variant).
Mobile overall performance:
Control: 4.4% conversion rate
Treatment: 6.8% conversion rate, which is a 54% increase with a 94% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased, this time by 161% with a 99% level of confidence.
Recurring performance:
Control: 0.55% conversion rate
Treatment: 0.92% conversion rate, which is a 66% increase with a 85% level of confidence. Recurring revenue per visitor also increased by 242%, with a 97% level of confidence.
One-time performance:
Control: 2.1% conversion rate
Treatment: 3.2% conversion rate, which is a 52% increase with a 98% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 62%, with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Does adding a monthly donate button in the main navigation impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a second monthly donate button for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/06/2024
In this test we aimed to increase the overall conversion rate by introducing two separate donation buttons, leading to distinct forms for one-time and recurring donations. We hypothesized that users would be more inclined to donate if they received a more explicit message about the available donation options, reducing ambiguity. Additionally, pre-determining the type of donation before entering the donation form would minimize friction during the process.
Our main hypothesis was that including an additional button specifically for recurring donations, in addition to the standard red donate button, is likely to have a positive impact on our overall conversion rate. Not only will it reduce the number of cancellations after the first donation, but it will also make the donation process more convenient for potential donors who prefer to make recurring contributions. This will ultimately lead to increased qualified donors and a higher conversion rate.
In this experiment we had 2 treatment variants; the first had a ‘donate’ button that led to the default combo form (one-time default), while the ‘give monthly’ button led to the same form, but with monthly pre-selected.
The second treatment had the same functionality for the ‘donate’ button, but the ‘give monthly’ button linked to a form that only offered recurring giving as the main option (there was a secondary link the form to give one-time).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Data analysis revealed that both variants substantially increased overall revenue and conversion rates. The improvements were primarily observed in desktop users, indicating that the changes implemented significantly impacted this segment. Significant results from this segment were:
Variant B:
-       +45% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop one-time conversion rate.
-       +126% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop one-time RPV.
-       +118% statistically significant lift (96%) in desktop recurring conversion rate.
Variant C:
-       +42% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop one-time conversion rate.
-       +92% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop one-time RPV.
-       +146% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop recurring conversion rate.
These results support our hypotheses that (1) Offering users two distinct donation buttons, leading to separate forms for one-time and recurring donations, can increase the conversion rate. (2) A more explicit message about the available donation options, with less ambiguity, can further improve the likelihood of user conversion. (3) Pre-determining the type of donation before entering the form may also reduce friction during the process.

experiments.title: Does adding a slide-up on the homepage impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a slide-up to the homepage for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates because of the principles of visual salience and immediacy. because  of the principles of visual salience and immediacy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2024 - 11/12/2024
This test explored the psychological impact of adding a slide-up donation module on the Homepage. The goal was to increase engagement and prompt recurring donations by creating a subtle yet persuasive nudge for visitors.
Our hypothesis was that by adding a slide up banner to the Homepage, we will increase donor conversion. This is because by providing users with the option to donate without having to click a donate button first, and then carrying their donation choice over to the donation form, we are reducing friction for the users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
+25% Increase in Recurring Donor Conversion Rate: Demonstrated a statistically significant lift at a 96% confidence level.
+54% Increase in Recurring Revenue per Visitor: Achieved a statistically significant lift with a 99% confidence level.
Overall donor conversion rates increased by 9.8% with a 96% level of confidence. When digging into the data, we saw some interesting trends:
Rev/visitor across the board increased by 1.9% with a 58% level of confidence.
Desktop:
Conversion rates increased by 8.4% with a 81% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased, albeit only by 3% with a 60% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Conversion rates increased by 11.9% with a 91% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased, albeit only by 3.7% with a 61% level of confidence.
It is when looking at one-time versus recurring gifts that we saw the biggest impact:
One-time:
The slide-up had a 6% increase in conversion rates, with a 78% level of confidence. The revenue per visitor was relatively flat, with only a 0.4% decrease and a 52% level of confidence.
Recurring:
The slide-up had a 29% increase in recurring gifts, with a 96% level of confidence. This also translated to a much higher revenue per visitor, with a 53% increase and a 99% level of confidence.
Behavioral Insights:
The slide-up module likely leveraged the principles of visual salience and immediacy, drawing users' attention without disrupting their browsing experience. By introducing a gentle prompt, the module created a sense of immediacy that encouraged users to act on their initial donation intent. Additionally, the default effect—where people tend to stick with recurring actions once initiated—likely contributed to the significant lift in recurring donor conversions and revenue.
The positive impact, particularly among recurring donors, suggests that the slide-up module reinforces commitment by subtly positioning the act of recurring donation as a socially desirable default behavior. The module's presence on the Homepage also supported mere-exposure effect principles, where repeated visibility increases familiarity and perceived trustworthiness of the donation option.
Next Steps:
We will test the slide-up module against the emergency banner for our next iteration. This will allow us to compare which feature better leverages urgency and social proof to maximize visitor engagement and conversion, further refining our understanding of behavioral triggers in the donation journey

experiments.title: Adding a Micro-Ask In An Emergency Banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a micro-ask decision in the emergency banner will effectively increase the overall conversion rates. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding a simple yes/no question on a banner could increase donor conversion on the page. By asking a prospective donor if they will donate and then giving them a choice of saying yes or no could increase the likelihood of donating because the donor has autonomy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into an emergency banner.
Key results from the analysis include:
+63% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop click rate.
+77% statistically significant lift (95%) in desktop conversion rate.
+174% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop RPV.
The +77% lift in desktop conversion rate primarily stems from increased clicks on the banner button. Notably, the variant exhibited a click rate lift of +63%. From a psychological standpoint, individuals are more inclined to engage when they feel a sense of control and agency over their actions. This empowerment instills a sense of investment in the decision-making process, thereby enhancing their likelihood to interact with the banner, manifested in increased clicks. Essentially, by offering users the freedom to choose, we create an experience that resonates with their preferences and motivations.
As a result, the observed click rate lift underscores this approach's efficacy in driving user engagement and, ultimately, achieving our desired outcomes. Furthermore, the +174% lift in desktop Revenue per Visitor (RPV) suggests a significant behavioral shift. Users exposed to the micro-ask demonstrated a heightened propensity for larger donations following engagement. While quantifying psychological shifts presents challenges, we hypothesize that engagement with the micro-ask fosters deeper commitment, leading to larger average donations. Partial validation of this hypothesis is observed in the directional lift in donation form conversion rates between treatments (37.5% versus 34.6%). These findings underscore a compelling insight: the introduction of a micro-ask substantially enhances user engagement despite its seemingly inconsequential nature.
Moreover, it appears to instill a sense of commitment, resulting in more substantial contributions. This indicates that when users encounter micro-asks, they not only exhibit a higher inclination to engage but also tend to make larger donations, driven by an increased sense of commitment post-engagement.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security and privacy of a donor’s payment information impacts conversion at point of transaction
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the perceived security and privacy for donors will achieve a positive lift in donor conversion because online security causes anxiety and friction to the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2023
We know that online security and privacy is highly important to people, especially when it comes to entering sensitive data like payment information online. But how important is online security and privacy really and what causes the greatest anxiety and friction around this topic in the giving process? To test into this, we developed an experiment on Save the Children’s main donation page that communicated how seriously we take data security.  Specifically the message said, “We take data and privacy seriously. Your payment information is secure and will be used only to process your gift.” This message was placed just under the donate button.
Additionally, in order to draw attention to the security statement below the donate button, we animated a lock icon to catch the eye of potential donors.
For this experiment, we split traffic evenly between the treatment and the control and ran it for a total of 33 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment that displayed a security statement and an animated lock icon resulted in an 11.1% increase in donations with a high level of statistical confidence. The key learning from this experiment is that donors DO have anxiety and friction when giving; even at the point of transacting, but when we can clearly communicate to them that their privacy and security is not only important to us, but tell them exactly what we will do with their payment information, we can remove some of that perceived cost in the donor's mind and increase their likelihood to give.
Other notable data points from the experiment were:
Overall mobile donor conversion was higher for the treatment (+16%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).Mobile recurring donor conversion was higher for the treatment (+56%) at a statistically valid LoC (99%). Mobile recurring revenue per visitor was higher for the variant (+83%) at a statistically valid LoC (99.8%).
As a whole, we believe that mobile users saw such a significant increase in conversion (more so than desktop) because the animated lock icon drew people's attention to the security statement. This animation and statement on mobile devices is much more prominent resulting in more people seeing it and ultimately converting at a higher rate.
Additionally, the dramatic lift in conversion and revenue for recurring donations only elevates the very real anxiety that people have around data security. For people considering making an ongoing gift to the organization, the weight of that anxiety is heavier as they consider and question what happens with their payment information every month as it gets charged for their recurring gift commitment.
This experiment resulted in an estimated revenue impact over seven figures for recurring donors.
Future experimentation could involve testing different variations of security statements and icons to find the most effective combination. Additionally, it may be worthwhile to test the impact of security messaging on different types of donors or donation amounts to see if the effect is consistent across different segments.

Continued testing is needed to really understand the motivation of this audience and what elements of friction exist in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How a micro-goal slide in impacts donor conversion during an emergency over an alert banner
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro goal for website visitors during an emergency  will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 03/09/2022
When emergency relief is needed somewhere around the world, Save the Children traditionally places an emergency alert banner on their site. The banner can’t be missed on the site because it takes up quite a bit of real estate, is bright red, and says “Emergency Alert”. Save has tested the copy that is on this banner in the past, but there hadn’t been significant testing around the tactical approach of driving urgency and conversion.
When the Ukraine crisis hit, Save saw an unprecedented amount of traffic to their website and wanted to see if they could find an another way to convert this high volume of traffic into donations. To test this, we developed a slide-in module that used a micro-goal of the number of donors needed this hour to help children in crisis. The micro-goal numbers in the slide in were dynamic and reset every hour. If a user clicked “donate now,” and then returned to the homepage within the hour, the number would be lower than what they saw previously. The slide in module was sticky and stayed in view when the user scrolled down the page and had the ability to be removed via clicking on the “x” in the top right corner, similar to the emergency banner. Both of these CTA’s drove to the same donation page for the control and treatment
experiments.keyLearnings:
The micro-goal slide in banner increase donor conversion in the five days that this ran by 21% (with a 99% LoC)! There was also a proportional decline in the number of undesignated purchases. This was of much interest, as users were directed to an undesignated form in both the variant and the control. Our assessment is that users shown the variant were better able to determine the slider was headed to a more general donation form – be it from a difference in copy (context/phrasing), the slider color, or slider location. The ability to identify form intent led variant users to seek out a form that matched their motivations (in this case, Ukraine), leading to an increase in emergency purchases and a decrease in undesignated purchases. As there was no difference in overall purchases, conversion rate, or revenue/visitor, there was possibly less potential friction for variant users. A few days into the test, there was a slight uptick in call center donors who requested their gifts be specifically designated for Ukraine. We shifted 100% of test traffic to the variant, as these users were more likely to give an emergency gift (potentially reducing call center calls). Shortly after, we shifted fundraising from Ukraine designated to CEF, which made it difficult to measure the impact of the shift.
We know that the emergency alert banner has worked in the past, but when users see a specific goal with a specific timeline/deadline, this increases the urgency of the ask and then increases the motivation and conversion of visitors.
Next steps: Additional testing is needed with this tactic and also the emergency alert banner to better identify if it was the micro goal or the slide in functionality or some other element that really led to the success of the variant.

experiments.title: How removing gift attribution information from the footer of the website impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What is the image of removing the gift attribution information from the footer of the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2021 - 07/20/2021
In the footer of the Save the Children website, there is an image and copy that communicates to website visitors what percentage of their gift goes towards the mission. This is placed above third party trust logos and shown across the entire Save the Children website. As internal discussion came up around what the actual percentage is that they can communicate to people about their gift going directly to the mission, they wondered how important that information actually is in the footer of the site. To test this, they simply removed the image of the chart showing the percentage and also the copy next to it that read, “When you give, 85% of every dollar goes straight to our mission. Find out more.” They tested this for five weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By removing the gift attribution image and copy from the footer of the website, donor conversion increased by 7%. This experiment highlights that this information isn't a critical factor in a person's decision to give - at least not having it in the footer of the website. In fact, from this experiment, we could believe that having the information in the footer of the website was actually causing friction in the giving process prior to removing it. From this experiment, a couple of scenarios could be true:
The percentage of a person's gift going directly towards the mission may have actually decreased a person's motivation to give. This visual reminder, and maybe even education to the web visitor, may have caused them to question why it is only 85%. Most people assume all of their gift is going directly towards the mission. So for prospective donors, this information didn't enhance their likelihood to give.This information wasn't just present in the footer of the homepage or informational pages, it was also present on the donation pages. So for the donor who had decided to make a gift, by showing this information at a point on the page that is close to the final call-to-action in their donation process may have caused friction in that pathway
This doesn't mean that this information isn't important or healthy to communicate to donors and prospective donors. But it does highlight how we should communicate this information and where in an effort to help improve donor conversion and not harm it.

experiments.title: How presenting a micro ask before the macro, impacts email conversion from a pop-up on the website
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a micro ask before the macro ask increase email acquisition from the pop-up on the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2021 - 05/27/2021
For the past five months, Save the Children has been testing into the email acquisition pop-up on their website. The majority of these experiments have been focused around messaging and value proposition. But all of these experiments have resulted in no positive or negative impact on conversion. As we continue to test into this feature on the website and look for ways to optimize the experience, we hypothesized that we might be able to engage more website users and ultimately increase name conversion by presenting an easier engagement experience on the pop-up before we ask them to give us their email address.
To test this, we presented a “yes”/”no” question that a user would answer before the email acquisition form field would show. The “yes”/”no” question was the same sentiment as the control – “Would you like to stay informed?”. The “Yes” button was highlighted red. When a person would click on the “Yes” button, an email form would slide out and the copy read, “Great! Please provide your email address to sign up.” If a person clicked “No”, the pop-up would simply disappear.
The control presented the same kind of email acquisition opportunity, but in the form of a statement – “Sign up to stay informed”.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 10 days, we found that the "Yes"/"No" micro ask increased email acquisition by 35%! This valid lift happened on both desktop and mobile devices. In fact, mobile devices had the most significant valid increase in name conversion of 42% (99% LoC). This experiment shows us the importance of engaging people through an easy entrance point results in more people likely to convert. This micro ask increases a persons likelihood and motivation to complete the macro ask.

experiments.title: How does cause-specific messaging on the donation form affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on different themes for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2025 - 07/28/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of focusing the donation form messaging around specific thematic areas of work. experiments.title: How increasing the one-time gift array on a Meta donation form impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing gift arrays for Meta donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2025 - 12/10/2025
This test evaluated the effect of increasing the one-time ask array on Meta forms. Our hypothesis was that keeping the end anchor amount of $150 but increasing the amounts between would increase average gift without negatively impacting conversion. We had previously tested the one-time pre-selected gift array for Meta; the test results can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine if increasing the middle amounts within a one-time ask array on Meta forms, while keeping the $150 end anchor, would increase the average gift value without decreasing conversion rates.
What Drove the Lift
Most donors default to the pre-selected amount, so the gains were concentrated among active choosers — donors who navigated away from the default. Two dynamics appear to have influenced their behavior:
The compromise effect: Donors tend to avoid extremes and gravitate toward a middle option. Raising the middle amounts nudged that "safe" choice upward.
 	Reduced sticker shock: Tighter gaps between amounts made the transition from lower to higher options feel more natural.
Next Steps
We'll be rolling out the variant and continuing to test — both around the specific amounts and which value is pre-selected as the default.

experiments.title: Does the placement of the payment module impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe moving the module to the end of the form for donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2025 - 04/21/2025
Due to a PCI update from our donation form vendor, the payment module now loads more slowly. We aimed to test where to place this module on the form to avoid adding friction. Our hypothesis was that placing the slow-loading module near the top would increase user friction and reduce conversions. To address this, we moved the module to the last step, giving it time to load.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of repositioning the PCI payment module within the donation form. We hypothesized that placing the module near the top of the form was causing friction due to its slow load time, leading users to encounter an unresponsive payment field before it fully rendered. By moving the module to the final step, we aimed to give it sufficient time to load before the user needed to interact with it.
Key Results:
Overall Conversion Rate: +18% lift (80% confidence, meaning there is an 80% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
 	Overall Revenue Per Visitor: +8% lift (64% confidence, meaning there is a 64% probability the observed lift is due to the change) — not statistically significant
Insights:
The improvement in conversion rate reflects a meaningfully smoother customer journey. Repositioning the payment module to the last step ensures it is fully loaded by the time users reach it, eliminating a key point of friction. Mobile results showed significant gains, while desktop RPV declined and warrants further investigation.
Recommendation & Next Steps
Based on these results, we have moved the module to the bottom of all top-trafficked donation forms and will adopt this placement as the default for new forms going forward.

experiments.title: How adding an option to donate through ACH impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ACH option for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2025 - 08/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the donation form’s payment section. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option, but would improve the user experience for those who would interact with it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding an additional payment method to the payment section on the donation form. We hypothesized that adding an additional button would not create friction for users who didn’t want to use the option but would create a better user experience for the users who would interact with it.

Key Results:

-       +10% non-statistically significant lift (81%) in overall conversion rate.

-       No change in overall RPV.

This test showed that by offering another familiar, often trusted option, we removed a potential barrier for donors who prefer or are accustomed to direct debit. Also, direct debit is often perceived as more secure and reliable, so the mere presence of an additional trusted option reduced friction for all users and made more donors comfortable completing the gift.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have implemented the direct debit option across all donation forms.

experiments.title: How updating the layout of the homepage to focus on content impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clearer navigation for website visitors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2025 - 10/06/2025
Save the Children’s homepage serves multiple purposes and often appears cluttered. In this experiment, we introduced larger image blocks with concise text to guide users to relevant content. The test evaluated the effect of placing navigation-oriented content blocks at the top of the homepage. We hypothesized that this change would streamline access, helping users reach their desired content more efficiently and, in turn, boosting engagement and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine the impact of adding navigation focused content blocks to the top of the homepage. We hypothesized that this would reduce friction by providing users with an easier way to reach the content they want most. This would then increase engagement and conversion rate.
Key Results:
12% non-statistically significant decrease (94%) in overall conversion rate.
 	18% statistically significant decrease (95%) in overall revenue per visitor.
This test showed that changing the homepage content blocks from news-related to navigational blocks reduced emotional engagement and urgency cues, leading to lower donor conversion. News content activates empathy and a sense of immediacy, prompting spontaneous giving, while navigational blocks encourage exploration rather than action.

experiments.title: How adding match math language to a donation form impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the impact of a match gift for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/22/2025 - 10/28/2025
Save the Children runs multiple match campaigns throughout the year; for their fall match campaign, we wanted to test whether adding in ‘match math’ to a donation form would help increase conversions. Our hypothesis was that by showing the impact of a donor’ gift through ‘math’– Your gift of $25 becomes $50 in impact— we will help users recognize the increased impact of their gift, which will increase conversion. This experiment had 3 variants, with a control and 2 treatments that had the match math either above or below the gift array selections.

-       26% non-statistically significant increase (88%) in desktop conversion rate for variant C.
-       68% statistically significant increase (98%) in desktop RPV for variant C.
This test shows that our hypothesis proved true for desktop users but added friction for mobile users. The additional copy on mobile led to a shift in layout each time they selected a new ask amount, which has been shown to correlate with higher abandonment.

experiments.title: Does changing the headline language on a donation form impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headings to a question for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2024 - 10/15/2024
This test explored the psychological impact of changing the headings for the steps on the donation form. The goal was to increase conversion and average gift by creating a sense of empowerment for the visitor through phrasing the headings as questions.
Our hypothesis was that changing the headings on the donation form will increase donor conversion. This is because when users are presented with a softer, more personalized heading, they are more likely to engage, and ultimately convert. Changing the headings to a question provides the user with more sense of control during their interaction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the headings on the donation form to questions did not result in an increase in donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the data and consider the statistical significance of the results before making any conclusions or changes based on an experiment. It may also be beneficial to test different variations of the headings or other elements of the donation form to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, considering factors such as the wording, tone, and placement of elements on the form could also impact donor conversion rates.

Overall, it is essential to approach experimentation with a mindset of continuous learning and improvement, using data-driven insights to inform decision-making and optimize the donation process for the best results.

experiments.title: How revamping email subscription button copy affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve a greater email conversion rate because it will create a more immediate response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/25/2022 - 03/04/2022
If we change the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency, email conversion will increase because users need to act immediately. We tested four different variants against our control language “join us” for this experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall email conversion was directionally higher for "Sign Up" and "Join Now."
• Mobile email conversion was directionally higher for "Join Now."
• Desktop email conversion was directionally higher for all variants.
• Of the five, "sign up" (13.7%) and "join now" (24.1%) had higher email capture rates.
• Mobile email conversion was higher for "join now" (19%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (64%).
• All four variants' desktop conversion was higher at a non-statistically valid confidence levels.
• Recommendation is not changing the HP email copy due to lack of statistical significance.
• We could potentially test "sign up" vs. "join now" button language in the future, to see if there is a more significant result with fewer variants since both had a more significant email conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding a micro-goal slider that stresses urgency to a sponsorship page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency for potential donors will achieve increase sponsorship conversion  because there is a clear CTA. because  there is a clear CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2022 - 03/28/2022
Adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency will increase sponsorship conversion because there is a clear CTA. When a user enters the Child List Sponsorship page in the variant, a popup will appear after 3 seconds on the page. It presents a variable number of lifeline children that need to be sponsored. The popup scrolls with the page and can be closed by the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Desktop donor conversion was higher for the variant (28%) at a statistically valid confidence level (99%).
• Recommendation is to add the slider to all sponsorship-related pages.
• Overall, the variant produced an increase (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (92%).
• RPV was higher for the variant (13%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (84%).
• Desktop RPV was higher for the variant (34%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (95%).
• Mobile donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (16%).
• Mobile RPV was slightly lower for the variant (-6%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (66%).
• Based on 2021 figures (assuming fixed traffic and average gift), we project an incremental lift of $76,000 annually. The net 60-month value of these donors is approximately $1.4M.
• Given the statistical significance in desktop donor conversion (28%) and limited directional downside on mobile, we recommend adding the slider to all sponsorship pages.
• Results may have had an even greater significance if not for the Ukraine emergency. The experiment was close to validating five days in, but highly motivated traffic related to Ukraine evened out the treatments.
• Additionally, we look to test different versions of copy and visual elements employed by the slider to optimize the experience further.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity with more descriptive menu language affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more descriptive language in a dropdown menu will achieve a greater click-through rate by providing a more personal motive to click.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2022 - 10/05/2022
If we change the language for “Join Team Tomorrow” in the navigation dropdown, there will be a greater click-through rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Test stopped after several weeks due to a lack of signifiant results
• No significant difference in the click-through rate for any of the three treatments. Directionally, "Find a Cause" had a higher CTR (31% lift 85% confidence level - possible retest in the future).
• The overall test did not generate sufficient clicks. The next test iteration will individually list the four focus areas and have direct links to their corresponding donation forms.
• The treatment version using language like "Find a Cause" was more successful in motivating users to click, suggesting that works better than a generic request like "Join Team Tomorrow".
• The results of this experiment show that changing the language for "Join Team Tomorrow" on the website navigation dropdown resulted in a 30.9% increase in clicks for all visitors with 85.1% statistical confidence.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by presenting a micro goal on a child sponsorship funnel impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing urgency and a need for people consider child sponsorship  will achieve an increase in conversion (sponsorships/donations).
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2022 - 03/28/2022
Save the Children give people the opportunity to sponsor a child and provide for their needs. There is a lot of choices that a prospective donor has to make when they land on the child sponsorship page. From deciding if they want to sponsor a child, to deciding what child they need to sponsor, it can be overwhelming to a user. And while there is urgency in a need to sponsor a child, that message isn’t adequately communicated on the page and in the funnel.
To test into this and see if we could increase the urgency within the funnel, we created a slide in banner that would appear after 3 seconds on the page. This banner/pop-up would present a variable number of children (a micro-goal) that needed to be sponsored “by midnight tonight”. The popup scrolls with the page and has the ability to be closed by the user. We showed this variation to 50% of site users and ran the experiment for five weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we increase child sponsorships with the micro-goal urgency banner by 12% at a 90% level of confidence. Additionally, the greatest impact on conversion was for desktop devices. Desktop devices saw a 27.5% increase in conversion with a 97% level of confidence. There was no positive or negative impact on mobile devices.
The largest lift was related to Lifeline Sponsorship purchases, where the treatment saw a 151% lift over the control (46 vs 18). This was to be expected, as engaging with the slider added a Lifeline child to the cart, but was confirmation that if we guide donors to this product, they will respond positively.
There was no statistical significance between the treatments by channel, location, or new vs returning visitors.
What all of this tells us is that by presenting this micro-goal to site visitors (and telling them the deadline to meet this goal was midnight tonight), increase the urgency of the need which increased the motivation of the user. We know urgency drives conversion, but to see this urgency used during a season where urgency isn't naturally present is good to know that we can create urgency in our funnels and that donors are, in fact, highly driven by a need with a goal and deadline.
Next steps: While we hypothesize the increase in conversion was caused by the urgency that the micro-goal slide in banner created, the slide-in banner also was a totally different and new experience from the control. To isolate some of the variables used in this experiment and better understand and optimize this experience, continued testing of these different elements is the logical next step. Specifically, the copy being used, the functionality of the slide-in of placing a child "in the cart" vs taking users to a learn more page, the visual elements of the slide-in, etc.

On desktop, moving the donation module into the content showed a strong (though not statistically significant) lift in conversion. This suggests that desktop users may benefit from more time and context before being asked to give.
Overall, placing the donation module further down benefits desktop users but significantly reduces mobile conversions.

experiments.title: How removing the dollar ($) sign from a gift array impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe removing the dollar sign for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2025 - 07/24/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the dollar sign from the ask array levels on the donation form. We hypothesized that the dollar sign signaled the users brain to enter into transactional mode rather than staying connected to the emotion of the cause. Removing the dollar sign would allow the user to stay connected to the mission and would thus increase their willingness to give, potentially at a higher level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We set out to test if the dollar sign adds friction for donors.
We thought seeing a “$” might feel transactional, deterring emotional giving. Removing it, we believed, could keep donors focused on the mission and increase giving.
What Happened:
The results told a very different story.
Instead of reducing friction, removing the dollar sign significantly hurt performance—especially on desktop. Both conversion rate and revenue per visitor dropped, in some cases dramatically.
This suggests the dollar sign isn’t a distraction. It’s actually doing something helpful: giving users clarity about what’s being asked of them.
Overall Performance:
Across all users, conversion dropped from 40.5% to 33%—a 17% decrease (52% confidence). Revenue per visitor declined even more sharply, falling 64% with 98% statistical confidence.
Desktop Performance:
The desktop saw the most dramatic impact. Conversion rate fell from 48.1% to 18%—a 62% decrease (95% confidence). Revenue per visitor dropped 75% (99% confidence).
It’s worth noting this segment had a small sample size (15 gifts), but the magnitude and consistency of the drop are hard to ignore.
Mobile Performance:
Mobile told a more mixed story. The conversion rate increased from 36.2% to 40.9% (a 13% lift), but with only 29% confidence, it is not statistically reliable.
At the same time, revenue per visitor dropped 58% (90% confidence), indicating that even when more users converted, they gave less.
One-Time Gifts:
For one-time donations, conversion declined from 32.4% to 24.2%—a 25% decrease (62% confidence). Revenue per visitor fell 69% (98% confidence), reinforcing the broader pattern: fewer gifts, and smaller ones.
Recurring Gifts:
Recurring performance showed a slight increase in conversion (8.1% to 9.1%), but with very low confidence (13%) and only 6 total gifts.
Revenue per visitor was essentially flat, decreasing just 0.97% (50% confidence). With such a small sample, these results aren’t directional.
Takeaway:
The main takeaway: keeping the dollar sign significantly improves donor clarity and response.
The dollar sign is not a barrier; it provides important clarity and guides users effectively through the donation process.
Without it, donors may feel uncertain, which can lower both participation and donation amounts.

experiments.title: Does refreshing the homepage value prop with topical messaging impact conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value prop and a donation module for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2025 - 09/22/2025
This test was the second iteration of the value prop box on the homepage. This time, we added the donation module back to the hero image (which is directly above the box) and slightly revised the value prop copy. In the last test, the donate button was pushed below the fold, so we hypothesized that adding the donation module would solve this issue and allow for the topical value prop to be the focus for users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test was the second iteration of the value prop box on the homepage. This time, we added the donation module back to the hero image (which is directly above the box) and slightly changed the value prop copy. In the last test, the donate button was pushed below the fold, so we hypothesized that adding the donation module would solve this issue and allow for the topical value prop to be the focus for users.
Key Results:
35% non-statistically significant decline (88%) in recurring conversion rate.
 	33% non-statistically significant decline (86%) in recurring revenue per visitor. This test showed that a topical value proposition likely created urgency and relevance, making more visitors feel that giving was timely and impactful.
While urgency drove immediate one-time giving, it may have framed the donation as a short-term crisis response rather than an ongoing need. When the value prop emphasizes immediacy, donors are more inclined to act once rather than commit long-term.
Recommendation/Implementation: Given our focus on recurring giving, we will not be implementing the variant.
Overall performance:

experiments.title: How updating a callout with value prop on a campaign page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the value prop more topical for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2025 - 09/01/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the current value prop on the homepage from being a general organization focused to topical focused. With changes on the ground in Gaza, we wanted to test whether referencing that situation would make an impact. We hypothesized that this would help the user connect more with the organization by presenting a topic relevant and important to them.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test evaluated the impact of shifting the hero value proposition from a general, organization-wide focus to a topical, issue-specific focus. The hypothesis was that surfacing a topic personally relevant to the user would strengthen their connection to the organization and drive higher engagement.
Key Results
Overall Conversion Rate: -14% decline (65% confidence) — not statistically significant
 	Revenue Per Visitor: -3% decline (55% confidence) — not statistically significant
Insights
The results suggest that a topical focus may inadvertently alienate users who don't feel personally connected to the featured issue. Rather than a narrow topic, users appear to prefer a holistic snapshot of the organization — who we are, what we do, and the breadth of our impact. A general value prop may better serve the range of motivations users bring when they arrive.
Recommendation & Next Steps:
We plan to retest this module with two adjustments: reintroducing the donation module within the hero and refining the copy. This will help us isolate whether the topical framing itself — or the absence of a clear giving prompt — was the primary driver of the decline.

experiments.title: How changing copy on a button for a landing page impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing button copy to be clearer for potential monthly donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2025 - 04/29/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to clearly communicate the action and improve conversion rates. The results showed that the CTA change improved user clarity and increased conversion rates. The results showed whether this change improved conversion rates, though specific outcome data is not provided here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It is important to note that the donation sample size (17 total) was very small as we were aiming for monthly donors. This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to set appropriate expectations for the user.

Key Results:

-       +76% non-statistically significant lift (75%) in overall conversion rate.

-       +99% non-statistically significant lift (89%) in overall RPV.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have changed the CTA copy on the landing page to DONATE TODAY.

 

Desktop performance:

Control: 2.3% conversion rate and $0.76 RPV.

Treatment: 3.9% conversion rate, which is a 69% increase with a 44% level of confidence. RPV increased by 29% with a 60% level of confidence.

 

Mobile performance:

Control: 1.5% conversion rate and $0.28 RPV.

Treatment: 2.8% conversion rate, which was an 81% increase with a 69% level of confidence. RPV also increased by 167% with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a slide-up versus a slide-down performs during a Giving Tuesday high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different slide-up/down tactic for potential donors will lead to an improvement in donation conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2025 - 11/30/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of implementing a slide‑down banner instead of a slide‑up banner during a peak giving moment. We hypothesized that the slide‑down banner would be more visually prominent, capture user attention more effectively, and increase conversion. The slide-up banner was the control in this instance, as we had previously tested it during other time periods, and it proved to be the winner.
In this experiment, the slide-up appeared after the user started scrolling down the page, and the slide-down appeared once the visitor went to the homepage, remaining at the top of the page as the user scrolled. Users could minimize the slide-down banner if they wished; even when minimized, a donation array continued to display at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results show a directional decrease in overall conversion and RPV. This suggests that the slide‑down banner may have been perceived as distracting and less visually appealing, ultimately leading to reduced conversion.

Key Results:

-       -10% non-statistically significant decrease (71%) in overall conversion rate.

-       -2% non-statistically significant decrease (54%) in overall RPV.

Overall analysis:

As show in the statistics above, the slide down treatment version had a 10% decrease in overall transaction conversion rates, with a 70% level of confidence. The treatment also had a slightly lower revenue per visitor, showing a 1.8% decrease with a 54% level of confidence.

 

Desktop versus mobile analysis:

Desktop:

Control had a 6.4% conversion rate

Treatment had a 6.1% conversion rate, which is only a 4.6% decrease with a 27% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased slightly on the treatment version, with a 10% increase and a 67% level of confidence in that result.

Mobile:

Control had a 4.6% conversion rate

Treatment had a 3.7% conversion rate, which is a 19% decrease with an 80% level of confidence. Moreover, revenue per visitor also declined on the treatment version by 39%, with a 97% level of confidence.

 

Recurring versus one-time analysis

Recurring:

Control: 0.69% conversion rate

Treatment: 0.71% conversion rate, which is a 3% increase with only an 8% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also was marginally impacted, with a 1% increase but only a 50% level of confidence.

 

One-time

Control: 4.8% conversion rate

Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 12% decrease with a 75% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also marginally decreased by 1% with a 51% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing gift arrays for high-value zip codes impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a gift array for potential mid-level donors will achieve an increase in average gift and conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2025 - 05/02/2025
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether removing the gift array altogether on a donation form that was being used in high-value zip codes would impact conversion rates and average gift.
The hypothesis was that by removing the ask array amounts from the form, users would not be influenced by the “suggested” amounts and would naturally input a higher amount in the open field. This would increase the average gift and the potential pool of mid-level donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we did not see any statistically-significant impact on either conversion rates or average gift for this experiment. There was a 3.5% decrease in conversion rates across the board, with a 34% level of significance, and a 2.8% decrease in revenue per conversion, with a 54% level of significance.

When breaking down the results by desktop and mobile, we also did not see any large impact:

Desktop:

Control: 30% conversion rate, $73.79 revenue per visitor

Treatment: 29.1% conversion rate (40% level of confidence) and a 2.8% decrease in revenue per visitor (54% level of confidence).

Mobile:

Control: 20.5% conversion rate, $16.72 revenue per visitor

Treatment: 4% decrease in conversion rate (25% level of confidence) and a 6.9% decrease in revenue per visitor (60% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How adding gift equivalencies to each individual ask array button impacts conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift equivalencies for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2025 - 06/12/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding equivalencies to the ask array section, where each gift amount would have a related ‘item’ that it could fund.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Adding gift equivalencies to the ask array section resulted in a 4.6% decrease in donations for all traffic.
 	The statistical level of confidence was only 40.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.
 	Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.
 	It is essential to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions from A/B experiments.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the results, such as the presentation of gift equivalencies and donor preferences.
 	Further testing with a larger sample size and adjustments to the gift equivalencies may be needed to draw more accurate conclusions about their impact on conversion rates and average gift.

experiments.title: Does reducing the amount of gift arrays from 6 to 4 impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing gift array options for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because of the reduction of friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/15/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of reducing the number of ask array options. We hypothesized that reducing the number of options would reduce the amount of friction for the user and thus increase the conversion rate. We tested this on the main evergreen donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reducing the number of gift array options on the donation form resulted in a 20.7% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is sufficiently high before making any conclusive decisions based on the results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the impact of reducing the number of options on the donation form by conducting more tests with a larger sample size to validate these findings. This will help us make more informed decisions on how to optimize the donation process for potential donors in the future.

experiments.title: How removing the ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the processing fee option for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 05/31/2025
Many non-profit donation forms have a ‘cover-the-processing-fee’ feature where a donor can choose to increase their gift to cover any processing fees the non-profit might have to pay their payment providers. For Save the Children US, we wanted to test whether removing this function had an impact on conversion rates and revenue. We ran this one one of their evergreen donation forms, so the results were not influenced by any specific campaign driving traffic to that form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the processing fee option for potential donors resulted in a 5.2% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 41.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
Moreover, when reviewing overall performance based on revenue per visitor, the treatment also had a decrease (this removes large gifts), with a 37% decrease in revenue per visitor with a 99% level of confidence. This, however, makes sense, as the 'tip-up' function adds revenue to the overall gift.
Desktop:
Control: 30% conversion rate
Treatment: 24.6% conversion rate, which is a decrease of 18% with an 88% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 34% with a 95% level of confidence.
Mobile:
Control: 14% conversion rate
Treatment: 17% conversion rate, which is a 20% increase with a 79% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor, however, deceased by 35% with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding animation to a donation button in the navigation impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding animation to the donate button for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
