Timeframe: 02/08/2024 - 02/15/2024
This test explored the impact of adding an animation to the donate button in the main navigation. The goal was to increase conversion by drawing the user’s eye to the donate button. Our hypothesis was that by adding an animation to the donate button in the navigation, it will increase conversions.
In this experiment we tested 2 different variants of animation– a ‘wiggle’ and a ‘bounce.’
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was an overall increase in engagement with the donate button, but this experiment did not validate either on conversion rate or revenue per donor.

Desktop:

Control: 3.8% click through rate, 3.1% conversion rate.

Treatment B (wiggle): 4.6% click through rate, 3.7% conversion rate (20% increase at 76% level of confidence), 4% increase in revenue per visitor (55% level of confidence).

Treatment C (bounce): 4.3% click through rate, 4% conversion rate (27% increase at an 88% level of confidence), 12% decrease in revenue per visitor (67% level of confidence)

 

Mobile:

Control: 1% click through rate, 3.2% donor conversion rate.

Treatment B (wiggle): 1.6% click through rate, 3.1% conversion rate (3% decrease at 14% level of confidence), 19% decrease in revenue per visitor (79% level of confidence)

Treatment C (bounce): 2% click through rate, 2.7% conversion rate (14% decrease at a 66% level of confidence), 20% decrease in revenue per visitor (79% level of confidence)

experiments.title: How prioritizing a specific gift on a gift catalogue page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing a specific gift for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2025 - 04/11/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of highlighting a “priority” product to the top of the gift catalogue all products page for Save the Children. The gift catalogue is something that STC has used over the years to generate support for their mission. In this treatment, we showed one product out of a possible five that we chose to highlight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by highlighting a specific gift in the gift catalogue, there was a decrease in donations for all traffic by 18.1%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 63.0%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Additionally, revenue per visitor also saw a decrease of 14%, but only with a 67% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How redesigning content blocks on the homepage impacted clicks.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning content blocks for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks on said blocks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 07/24/2024
This test explored the impact of redesigning the content blocks on the Homepage. The goal was to increase clicks (engagement) and conversion by creating a more enticing and easier to navigate look to the content. We changed copy to be in white box overlaying image, with CTAs presented as links and highlight teal when hovering. CTA links always teal on mobile.
Our hypothesis was that redesigning the content blocks on the homepage will make them more engaging for the visitor and will lead to higher clicks and ultimately higher donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesigned content blocks on the homepage did not lead to more clicks from visitors. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control version. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

This experiment also did not impact donor conversion rates:

Control: 5%

Treatment: 4.9%, which was a 1.3% decrease with a 53% level of confidence.

 

The differences between desktop and mobile were also relatively small:

Desktop

Control: 2.4% click through rate; 5.2% donor conversion rate

Treatment: 2.4% click through rate (1.1% increase with a 21% level of confidence); 5.2% donor conversion rate (0.38% decrease, 11% level of confidence).

 

Mobile

Control: 2.6% click through rate; 4.9% conversion rate.

Treatment: 2.5% click through rate (1.6% decrease with a 34% level of confidence); 4.7% conversion rate (2.3% decrease with a 62% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How adding a wiki-style donation ask impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donation header for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2023 - 12/13/2023
This test was run to validate whether or not users on engagement pages are more likely to donate when there is a donation/ask box present at the top of the page. The rationale behind the test was based on the research that shows users who are actively engaging with our content are more likely to donate when directly presented with an ask as this reduces the barrier to entry and provides the user with an easy next step to take with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donation header for potential donors led to a 33.4% increase in donations for all traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence at 75.5% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making.

When digging deeper into the results, we saw:
One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (19.4% more) but only with a 51% level of confidence.
 	Revenue per visitor was 153% higher (albeit only $0.13 per visitor) for the treatment version, with an 88% level of confidence.
 	No recurring gifts on the control, but 4 on the treatment.
 	On desktop:
 	Control had a 0.19% conversion rate with a $0.07 rev/visitor
 	Treatment had a 0.06% conversion rate, which is a 65% decrease with a 99.4% level of confidence. It also had a 9% lower revenue per visitor, with a 57% level of confidence.
 	On mobile:
 	Control had a 0.09% conversion rate, with a $0.01 revenue per visitor.
 	Treatment had a 601% increase in conversion rate (albeit, 4 gifts compared to 29, so not a valid sample size) with a 99% level of confidence. Mobile also had a 2,512% increase in revenue per visitor (but from $0.01 to $0.34) with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using an open amount field on a donation form impacts mid-level conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for potential mid level donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to see whether mid-level donors would respond more to a donation form with an open field instead of the standard gift array. The hypothesis was that by removing the ask array amounts from the form, users would not be influenced by the “suggested” amounts and would naturally input a higher amount in the open field. This would increase the average gift and the potential pool of mid-level donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing ask array amounts on a donation form for mid-level donors did not result in an increase in conversion rates and average gift as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.5% decrease in donations with a statistical confidence level of 34.5%, indicating that these results are not reliable or valid for decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequately large to increase the statistical confidence level. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of donation forms, including those with ask array amounts and open fields, to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations from mid-level donors.

Overall, it is crucial to interpret results carefully and avoid making decisions based on inconclusive data. Further testing and refinement of donation forms may yield more reliable insights for optimizing fundraising efforts in the future.

experiments.title: How does the placement of an employer match widget impact donation conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro ask for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.

Timeframe: 05/29/2024 - 07/10/2024
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into the employer match component on donation forms. Our hypothesis was that adding a micro-ask decision to the donation form for the employer match section will increase revenue per visitor and overall matching funds. This is because when users are presented with a Yes/No decision, they are more likely to engage, for the good or bad, rather than to bypass it by scrolling. Incorporating a micro-ask can make users more involved and attentive toward the employer match.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of integrating a micro-ask into the employer match component on donation forms. Key results from the analysis include:

+4% non-statistically significant lift (68%) in overall conversion rate.

+9% non-statistically significant lift (87%) in overall revenue per visitor.

Directionally, we saw a lift in overall performance, but more importantly, we do not see a negative impact. We will be implementing this feature across all donation forms.

Results in detail:

Desktop:

Control: 14.5% conversion rate. Revenue per visitor of $20.06.

Treatment: 16.2% conversion rate, 96.5% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor of $19.79, a decrease of 1.3% with a 55% level of confidence.

 

Mobile:

Desktop: 7.1% conversion rate, $4.13 revenue per visitor.

Treatment: 6.8% conversion rate, $5.25 revenue per visitor, an increase of 27% with a 98% level of confidence

experiments.title: Does adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding other ways to give for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2024 - 09/10/2024
The goal of this test was to assess the impact of adding additional ways to give, specifically Crypto, on donation forms. We added some language to the bottom of the form, under the ‘donate’ button, that gave donors links to be able to give through their preferred vehicle.
Our hypothesis was that adding other ways to give at the bottom of a donation form will make it easier for people to donate in their preferred method. This will decrease friction for the users looking for these options without impacting the overall conversion of the donation form for all other users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding additional ways to give at the bottom of a donation form did not result in the intended outcome of increasing conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group, although the statistical confidence level was not high enough to draw definitive conclusions.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of adding new elements to a donation form and to carefully assess whether these changes align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable and conclusive results.

Moving forward, it would be beneficial to conduct further research and testing to better understand the impact of offering different ways to donate on conversion rates. This could include gathering feedback from donors, conducting surveys, and running additional A/B tests with a larger sample size and more rigorous statistical analysis.

experiments.title: Does adding options to donate by crypto and DAF impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding crypto and DAF giving options for donation page visitors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2024 - 12/02/2024
This test explored the impact of adding DAF and Crypto buttons to the donation form. The goal was to increase the use of these payment methods without detracting from regular form conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding crypto and DAF giving options to the donation page did not have a significant impact on conversion rates. The treatment actually saw a slight decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 44.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid for making future decisions. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that sample sizes are sufficient to provide reliable results and that the statistical significance threshold is met before drawing conclusions. It may also be worth exploring other variables or factors that could potentially impact donation conversion rates and testing them in a controlled manner to determine their effectiveness.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase sponsorship conversion by prioritizing a child on the sponsorship page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/03/2021
Save the Children has thousands of children that people can choose to sponsor at any given time. As users navigate throughout the site, they can sort and filter on certain variables to find the child they want to sponsor: location, gender, age, and by birth month. Many people used the search widgets to filter through children, because the number of children available was overwhelming—even after filtering through multiple searches. In an effort to make the decision process easier for an end user, we hypothesized that prioritizing a single child at the top of the page may increase conversion. To test into this, we developed a treatment that pulled out a single child, that remained static, that used personal language and information of that child. The variation said, “Sponsor Me!” and gave users the ability to quickly add that child to their cart to begin the sponsorship. All sponsorship cart functionality of the variation remained consistent with the control, the variation simply just visually highlighted one child at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we weren't able to validate the results, we did see a directional lift in conversion from the treatment variation. Additionally, in the initial weeks of the experiment running, the results were, in fact, positively valid. As the experiment continued to run, the results remained positive, but loss the strength of its validity. That said, this experiment reveals something interesting about the sponsors: while we might think that they want more options to choose, they actually are more likely to choose a child when we choose the child for them. The is a result of removing friction to the decision process.

One other result of this experiment is that we also saw a directional lift in overall purchases from this page. Yes, sponsorship conversion is the primary goal of this page, but it is important to ensure that any tactic we would apply to the page to impact that metric doesn't negatively impact the overall conversion.

Because we initially saw valid results with this experiment, which then turned to directional, we believe that this tactic can improve sponsorship conversion, but the principle may need to be strengthened through the value proposition of the prioritized child. This is the next phase of testing for this feature in an effort to validate the directional results.

experiments.title: How using short, “concern”, communal language in a pop-up impacts email conversion

rows.name: YOU ARE RIGHT TO BE CONCERNED

experiments.researchQuestion: How will short, “concerned”, communal language impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 03/25/2021
In a continued effort to better understand the motivations of site users and people converting on the forced pop-up on the STC website, we tested communal language again – but this time is was much shorter, used “concern” language, and had a similar tone of the call-to-action. This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for nine days, there was no difference in conversion and no sign of the percentage of validity increasing either. The good news is that we didn't see any negative impact on email conversion. That said, the motivation of people seeing and converting on this pop-up is extremely high, and even when trying to reinforce how a person feels - that they are "concerned" - we aren't able to influence their decision to convert or get more people to convert.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by removing gift amount options from a gift array affects donation rates

rows.name: 6 ask array choices for RD and 1X

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of ask arrays for potential donors will achieve greater conversion rates because we are eliminating options to decrease indecision and reduce friction from the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
We tested this on the GAF desktop on both monthly v. one-time gifts to see if decreasing the ask arrays would eliminate indecision and increase donor conversion since we are giving fewer choices to potential donors. For RD we removed the $75 and $100. For 1X – Removed $150 and $300, added $250.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:

• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (+12) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).

• Recurring donor conversion and RPV experienced directional declines.

• Recommendation is not to change the desktop ask array values.

• This test was the second iteration of ask array reduction tests. The first, tested mobile and yielded a strong statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How an animated donate button impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with an animated donation button in the primary navigation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/19/2021
With the high volume of traffic to the Save the Children website, we wanted to discover new ways to increase traffic to the donation page from the donate button in the primary navigation. To do this, we hypothesized that drawing more attention to the button might increase the number of people who would see the button and ultimately click and give. We set up a treatment version of the donate button that was animated. The animation was subtle with a motion going across the button coming from a heart icon. We tested this button against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The animated donation button ran for three weeks. While the goal was to increase donor conversion from the animated donate button - which we weren't able to do with valid results, we did see a significant lift in clicks from it (11%). Essentially, more people clicked to the page but only a minimal amount more donated.

When analyzing the donor conversion, we saw a higher conversion rate from site visitors if they landed on the donation page and saw the animated button versus landing on the homepage.

We also saw the greatest increase in conversion from mobile users. This is most likely due to the button being much more visible on the small device.

After running this experiment and seeing that it was likely not going to validate, we came away with two questions:
Will the subtle animation be a novelty feature that will fade with time?
 	Can we present the subtle animation to people when they are at a point in their experience when they are more likely to convert? This question leads us to our next experiment for this feature where we will trigger the animated donate button at a higher motivation point in their conversion pathway so we can not only get more people to the donation page, but get more people to give.

experiments.title: Will adding a sponsorship countdown slider increase donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that if we add a count down clock to the slide out for potential donors it will achieve a greater response rate and higher conversions because we are evoking a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2022 - 07/07/2022
We decided to test a slide out on the main website to determine if adding a countdown clock vs. our original it would increase the conversion rate with our audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:

• Test stopped due to lack of significant differences in donor conversion and RPV.

• Donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (+7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).

• Revenue per visitor was slightly higher for the variant (+5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (61%).

• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include a countdown clock.

experiments.title: How humanizing an appeal by presenting a specific child’s name, age, location and image for sponsorship affects conversion rates

rows.name: Slider with generic # of sponsors needed

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the featured child will outperform the slider  for users because they will identify with specific child details, more than a generic number of children in need of a sponsor, which will achieve a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2022 - 10/12/2022
We wanted to test a slide out with a specific number of children left in need of sponsorship by midnight vs. a (rotating) specific child above the search functionality that a potential donor could choose to sponsor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to declines in donor conversion and RPV.
• Donor conversion was lower for variant (-7%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-33%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.9%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slide.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by adding a countdown timer to a slide-out affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that if we add a count down clock to the slide out for potential donors it will achieve a greater response rate and higher conversions because we are evoking a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2022 - 07/07/2022
We decided to test a slide out variant on the child list page on all devices to determine if adding a countdown clock to our original # of kids still needing to be sponsored would increase the conversion rates with our audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant differences in donor conversion and RPV.
• Donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (+7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).
• Revenue per visitor was slightly higher for the variant (+5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (61%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include a countdown clock.
• We could potentially run this test during a high urgency campaign to see if it pulls a more valid response.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including the word “now” to a donate button affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve greater conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/19/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, there will be an increase in conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Click rate on the donate button was higher for the variant (11%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Overall donor conversion was higher for the variant (24%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (95%).
• RPV was slightly higher for the variant (1%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (53%).
• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (79%).
• One-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (58%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (40%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (85%).
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (69%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (96%).
• As the hero already uses "Donate Now", we recommend not changing the main donate button despite the significant life in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How replacing an “emergency” banner with an “emergency” slide-in affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage for potential donors will achieve a greater response than the emergency banner  because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 03/09/2022
Adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage will outperform the emergency banner because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
Numbers in the slide-in module are dynamic and reset every hour. If a user clicks “donate now” and then returns to the homepage within the hour, the number will be lower than what they saw previously. The slide-in module is sticky and stays in view when the user scrolls down the page and can be removed by clicking on the “x” in the top right corner, similar to the emergency banner. Both of these CTA’s are driving to an undesignated GAF form. Copy approved by legal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall increase in donor conversion on the homepage for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (50%).
• Overall increase in RPV on the homepage for the variant (4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (80%).
• Undesignated donor conversion was lower for the variant (-11%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.8%).
• Undesignated RPV declined for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (81%).
• Emergency donor conversion was higher for the variant (21%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.9%).
• Emergency RPV was higher for the variant (14%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (98%).
• A few days into the test, there was an uptick in call center donors who requested their gifts be assigned explicitly to Ukraine.
• We shifted 100% of test traffic to the variant, as these users were more likely to give an emergency gift (potentially reducing call center calls). Shortly after, we shifted from Ukraine designated CEF, which made it difficult to measure the impact of the shift.

experiments.title: How including the word “active” in an email acquisition form headline affects sign-up rates

rows.name: "Join us to learn how you can help make change for children."
"Be an active supporter. Join below to lean more."

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language to “active” text above the homepage email capture module  for potential donors will achieve a greater email conversion rate from users on the site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2022 - 09/06/2022
Changing the language above the homepage email capture module to see if adding active text increases email conversion from users on the site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• No significant positive differences in email conversion (by channel, new/return visitor, or location).
• Variant directionally underperformed (-11%) compared to the control (69% LoC).
• Recommendation is not to change the language above the homepage email module.
• Overall email conversion was lower for the variant (-11%) at anon-statistically validLoC (69%).
• Mobile email conversion was lower for the variant (-20%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (91%).
• Desktop email conversion was higher for the variant (23%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (65%).
• U.S. email conversion was slightly higher for the variant (7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (38%).
• International email conversion was lower for the variant (-51%) at a statistically valid LoC (99.7%).
• This test aimed to determine whether adding “Active” language above the homepage email module would increase email conversion. There were directional declines in email conversion across mobile, international, and new/ returning visitors. The only conversion lifts were seen for desktop (23%) and U.S.(7%) visitors, both of which were directional.
• We recommend not changing copy above the homepage email module

experiments.title: How simplifying an appeal by removing specific impact equivalences from a donation form affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation dollar amount equivalencies on donation forms for donors will achieve a greater conversion rate because  it’s more direct to the point.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2022 - 08/22/2022
This was tested on both desktop and mobile variants to determine if removing the presence of equivalencies would increase donor acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Overall donor conversion was lower for the variant (-2%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (32%).
• Overall RPV was higher for the variant (10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (79%).
• Mobile RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (96%).
• Future tests will examine removing the equivalencies exclusively for mobile users and changing them to reflect recurring values instead of one-time.
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (31%) at anon-statistically valid LoC (92%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (16%) at anon-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• One-time donor conversion was lower for the variant (-7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• One-time RPV was higher for the variant (+10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (78%).
• Mobile donor conversion was higher for the variant (9%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (66%).
• Mobile RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (96%).
• Mobile recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (50%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (93%).
• Mobile recurring RPV was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (86%).
• Mobile one-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (13%).
• Mobile one-time RPV was higher for the variant (36%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).
• Desktop donor conversion was lower for the variant (-10%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (84%).
• Desktop RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (60%).
• Desktop recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).
• Desktop recurring RPV was lower for the variant (-1%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (51%).
• Desktop one-time donor conversion was lower for the variant (-14%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (92%).
• Desktop one-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (59%).
• The data shows that removing equivalencies specifically favored recurring donor conversion on mobile devices.
• Recommendation is not to remove equivalencies on donation forms.

experiments.title: How changing donate button copy to convey greater clarity and urgency affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve an increase in click rate because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 02/23/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, clicks will increase because donors need to act immediately. We examined how different CTA’s affected the click rate on the main donation button. There were four variants with differing language; testing the verbiage of donate v. give v. help in addition to temporal urgency now v. today. This test exclusively measured click rate and did not delve into conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped for lack of significant results after 2 1/2 weeks.
• Lowest performing treatment was "give now" (-1.6%) with a confidence of (9%).
• Best performing treatment was "donate today" (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence of (64%).
• Further tests will examine the impact on donor conversion and click rate.

experiments.title: How adding value with more descriptive impact language affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that improving the value proposition with more descriptive language will achieve a higher click-through rate by clarifying the impact and outcome of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/31/2022 - 09/26/2022
We wanted to know if replacing non-descript, program-focused language with outcome-focused language affected would improve the value proposition in the minds of web traffic and give them more motivation to click through and learn more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that using more descriptive, outcome-focused language to promote a program has the potential to increase click-through rates (CTR) by communicating more value to prospects and thus giving them a better reason to learn more by clicking through.
Treatment one asks "Do you believe children deserve a better future? Join the team" This not only implies the outcome of joining but also aligns the offer with the prospect's values, further compelling them to act and producing a 36.4% increase in click-through rates with a 76% level of confidence.
Treatment two was less specific than the control, simply stating that a monthly donation can make a difference without communicating what that difference is or why it matters and producing an 18% decrease in clicks.
And while the 36.4% increase in clicks created by treatment one was not statistically valid, we know that there is little risk in rolling out a treatment that has shown a lift. We therefore recommend rolling this treatment out to 100% of traffic.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security on a donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding visual elements to the credit card section increase perceived security and ultimately donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2021 - 07/21/2021
There are many elements of friction on a donation page. One of the most significant areas where friction can be found on these pages is the credit card section. Prospective donors are asked to provide their financial information in this section which can cause anxiety for people during the giving process. We hypothesized that visually telling the donor that their information was secure may impact their liklihood to complete the donation process. To test into this, Save the Children added a padlock image and border around the credit card section to visually communicate to people that their information is safe, it’s secure.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall transactions, there was no impact on donor conversion. It is important to keep in mind that this experiment was executed on the primary donation page where differing motivations from all channels types are pushing traffic. While there wasn't any impact - positive or negative - on overall donor conversion, specific segments did see a significant impact.
On mobile devices, which makes up 60% of Save the Children's traffic, the perceived security feature increased donor conversion by 10% at a 96% level of confidence. As we know, the donor experience on mobile devices is very different from desktop. With the smaller device, everything is a bit more prominent - which would make the visual elements added to the form even more apparent and easing the minds of people as they were giving on their phones. These results also bring up an interesting hypothesis that mobile users may be more concerned about the safety of their information than desktop users. This sounds counterintuitive, but so much is being done now on commerce sites to make purchasing so much easier that people normally don't even have to think about that kind of stuff. The visual reminder may have brought this to their attention and caused them to feel more at ease, confirming in their mind that this was, in fact, secure.
We also saw an increase in giving for returning visitors. There was a 13% increase in donor conversion at a 92% LoC. While this didn't validate for this segment, the results were directional and indicate that they will, more than not, have a positive response to the higher perceived security. It is interesting that new visitors weren't impacted in the same way. Without knowing if these return visitors were previous donors or not, what we can learn from this is that the visual security elements may have been the motivating factor to tip the balance for returning visitors in their decision to actually make a gift.
Because there is no negative impact on any segment or channel through this experiment, and some segments will - with confidence - see an increase in donor conversion, we propose rolling this feature out among all donation pages.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will expanding the value proposition to emphasize the impact the donor can have by making a gift, increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2021 - 02/12/2021
During an analysis of the Save the Children (STC) primary donation page, a research panel noted that the page provided minimal details on the history of the organization and why donations to STC are important. When we expanded this value proposition based upon information found in other areas of the site, the perceived appeal of the information and likelihood for them to make a donation increased significantly.
With this in mind, we hypothesized that testing new value proposition copy on the primary donation page based upon the copy used on the “Why Save the Children” page of the website might increase donor conversion on the donation page. The goal with the new, expanded value proposition was to emphasize the impact the donor would have when making a donation and how much their donation was needed to help children around the world.
This experiment split the site traffic in half from all channels going to and landing on the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for four weeks, we found no overall difference in conversion rate from the expanded value proposition. Because the primary donation page gets traffic from many different channels and motivations of people, it's important to look at what happened within individual segments to better understand the learnings and results from this experiment.
When the homepage was the entry point to the donation page, we saw a slight lift in conversion (5% with an 89% LOC). This data segment had the highest LOC from the entire experiment.
When the donation page was the entry point, the only increase in conversion (though not statistically valid) was for mobile devices and new users. This aligns with the fact that new users tend to need more information and a stronger value proposition before they decide to give. It also highlights a potential hypothesis around traffic coming directly to the primary donation page from other channels is already highly motivated and the expanded value proposition - that took a softer, more donor-centric approach - created more of a barrier for the motivated traffic then helped them convert.
It's important to note that there was no statistically valid negative impact on conversion with the expanded value proposition variation. This tells us that there are more audiences and segments who positively responded to the value proposition.
Because of this, and the trend we saw in prospective donors coming from channels that most likely had harder hitting call-to-actions and were sent directly to the donation page, we propose testing the expanded value proposition again but nuanced with stronger language and CTA's to reflect the more conversion-centric audience.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.
